name: Amy Beliveau
background: Beliveau was described as a newcomer to the Planning Board race, a six-year Winchester resident, and a Town Meeting member from precinct seven. In her candidate profile, she cited more than a decade of experience in development, design, and negotiation, along with service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee.
summary: Beliveau won one of two Planning Board seats in 2026. Her campaign emphasized balanced growth, real-estate and development experience, data-driven decision-making, and collaboration to align housing growth with Winchester’s history and goals.
locationNote: Winchester resident; Town Meeting member representing precinct seven.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting and town committee experience
priorRecordSummary: She did not appear in the source packet as a prior Planning Board member, but she cited service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee, plus professional development and real-estate experience relevant to board work.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities position in provided sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources focus on planning, housing, zoning, and development rather than school building issues.

stanceCards.label: Development-oriented, data-driven planning
stanceCards.detail: Beliveau said she would bring real-estate and development knowledge, understanding of project finance, and a data-driven approach to help the town create value and achieve its objectives.

stanceCards.label: Supports adding housing options
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, she said Winchester must add housing and urged looking beyond transit-oriented corridors, including possible interior conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines experience and approach
evidence.summary: Beliveau said Winchester should grow in a balanced and thoughtful way and highlighted over a decade of experience in development, design, negotiation, and town service on Town Meeting, the EFPBC, and the State of the Town Committee.

evidence.title: Forum: housing growth and preservation
evidence.summary: At a League of Women Voters forum, Beliveau said she brings real-estate industry knowledge the board lacks and argued the town should consider housing options beyond MBTA Communities corridors, including conversions of large homes in single-family neighborhoods.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Beliveau received 2,042 votes in the Planning Board race, finishing second in a two-seat contest behind Keri Layton and ahead of Nicholas Rossettos.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage confirms win
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that Beliveau unseated incumbent Nicholas Rossettos to claim one of two open Planning Board seats.

name: Heather von Mering
background: Mother of five in Winchester Public Schools; vice president at Von Salmi & Associates working in construction and asset planning; longtime Winchester town leader with 16 years of service including the Historical Commission, Planning Board, Capital Planning Committee, Town Moderator, and Town Meeting Member.
summary: Von Mering ran for School Committee in 2026 emphasizing parent experience, literacy reform, special education advocacy, facilities and capital-planning expertise, and municipal finance experience. Her public record in the source packet is strongest on literacy, special education, school budgeting, and facilities stewardship. She lost the 2026 race to John Bellaire.
locationNote: Winchester; moved to town in 2009 according to forum coverage.
priorRecordLabel: Town government and school-budget advocacy record
priorRecordSummary: Her documented record is mainly outside the School Committee itself: 16 years in town government roles, service as Town Moderator and Town Meeting Member, and a 2025 Town Meeting amendment supporting the full requested school budget with additional free cash rather than relying on Circuit Breaker funds.
keyThemes: Municipal finance and transparent public process
Cautious approach to taking on new school commitments before closing achievement gaps

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: She described herself as a longtime literacy reform advocate, backed the district’s new literacy curriculum, and focused on foundational literacy instruction, Tier 2 supports, and earlier identification of dyslexia and language-based learning disabilities.

stanceCards.label: Backed full school budget request
stanceCards.detail: At 2025 Town Meeting, she argued Town Meeting should fund the school budget as requested and proposed adding $1.7 million in free cash to offset the deficit instead of using Circuit Breaker funds.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes fiscal stewardship
stanceCards.detail: She tied her capital-planning and town-government experience to disciplined goal setting, transparency, facilities governance, and municipal finance.

stanceCards.label: Facilities planning background
stanceCards.detail: Her construction and asset-planning work informed her stated focus on stewardship of school buildings and long-term capital planning.

stanceCards.label: Defers to expertise on tech policy
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, she said the School Committee should rely on outside expertise on artificial intelligence and that writing technology policy without expertise would be inappropriate.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines parent, advocacy, professional, and town-government experience
evidence.summary: Von Mering said she would bring perspectives as a parent of five in Winchester schools, a literacy and special education advocate, a construction and asset-planning professional, and a longtime town leader with experience in municipal finance and public process.

evidence.title: Forum coverage highlights literacy and special education advocacy
evidence.summary: At the School Committee forum, she said she had advocated for curriculum changes since at least 2017, helped organize families of children with dyslexia, and argued for early identification and support because earlier intervention lowers later costs.

evidence.title: Forum response on artificial intelligence policy
evidence.summary: She said she was not an expert on AI and that the School Committee should rely on outside expertise rather than trying to craft technology policy alone.

evidence.title: Town Meeting amendment supporting full school budget
evidence.summary: In 2025, von Mering moved to amend the school budget to the School Committee recommendation and add $1.7 million in free cash, saying she did not support using Circuit Breaker funds for the FY26 budget and that the best path was to fund the school budget as requested.

evidence.title: Election-night summary of campaign message
evidence.summary: Election-night coverage said von Mering argued the district should close literacy and math gaps before taking on new commitments; it also noted both candidates called for greater attention to student mental health and said Winchester lacked a coherent technology policy.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show von Mering received 1,861 votes to John Bellaire’s 2,192 in the School Committee race.

name: John Bellaire
background: Winchester High School graduate and Brown University graduate who studied education and public policy. He works for Bellwether, a national education nonprofit focused on state school finance, and has described experience as an education researcher, youth mentor, substitute teacher, and Weekend Mentor for Winchester A Better Chance.
summary: Bellaire ran for School Committee in 2026 emphasizing education policy expertise, school finance research, literacy improvement, student engagement, classroom technology, and student mental health. He won the one open seat in the March 21, 2026 town election.
locationNote: Winchester Public Schools graduate; described himself as a graduate of Muraco Elementary, McCall Middle, and Winchester High School.
priorRecordLabel: Education policy and local youth mentorship experience
priorRecordSummary: The available sources show professional experience in education research and school finance, work with Winchester A Better Chance, and substitute teaching at Vinson-Owen Elementary, but do not show a prior elected record on the School Committee.
keyThemes: literacy improvement and earlier student support

stanceCards.label: Supports stronger literacy work
stanceCards.detail: Said Winchester has been 'behind the curve and a bit delayed' on literacy and pointed to rising shares of students not meeting grade-level expectations.

stanceCards.label: Focus on budget analysis
stanceCards.detail: Highlighted his background developing financial models for equitable and transparent school finance systems and cited local special education spending data.

stanceCards.label: Supports student mental health
stanceCards.detail: Said his experiences as a Winchester student motivate him to support student mental health.

stanceCards.label: Wants attention to classroom technology
stanceCards.detail: Said his student experience motivates him to address technology in the classroom.

evidence.title: Candidate profile describes education research and mentoring background
evidence.summary: Bellaire said he works at a leading education nonprofit conducting research and financial modeling on equitable and transparent school finance systems, conducts school quality reviews, mentors Winchester ABC scholars, and previously worked as a substitute teacher at Vinson-Owen Elementary.

evidence.title: Candidate profile lists priorities shaped by his experience as a Winchester student
evidence.summary: Bellaire said his experiences balancing academics, arts, athletics, and extracurriculars motivate him to provide high-quality student engagement opportunities, address technology in the classroom, and support student mental health.

evidence.title: Forum coverage highlights literacy views
evidence.summary: At the candidate forum, Bellaire said Winchester has been 'behind the curve and a bit delayed' on literacy and cited worsening MCAS reading performance since 2019.

evidence.title: Forum coverage cites early support and special education cost concerns
evidence.summary: Bellaire said roughly 30% of the recommended School Committee budget goes toward special education and argued it is better to address student needs early rather than waiting for students to fall behind.

evidence.title: 2026 town election results
evidence.summary: Official results show John H. Bellaire received 2,192 votes to Heather R. von Mering's 1,861 for School Committee, winning the one seat.

name: Keri Layton
background: Layton said she began civic work in Winchester as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member in 2017, served on the Master Plan Steering Committee, then served three years on the Conservation Commission before joining the Planning Board. She chaired the board during North Main Street rezoning and said she is seeking a second term.
summary: Incumbent Planning Board member with prior Town Meeting, Master Plan, and Conservation Commission experience. Her profile and forum comments emphasize housing production, zoning implementation, environmental considerations, and mixed-use development that can broaden the tax base.
locationNote: Winchester; previously a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member and later listed in 2026 election results for Precinct 3 Town Meeting member.
priorRecordLabel: Incumbent Planning Board member and board chair
priorRecordSummary: Layton described serving on the Planning Board, chairing during MBTA Communities Act zoning implementation and North Main Street rezoning, and participating in review of the 10 Converse Place / 33 Mount Vernon Street redevelopment application.

stanceCards.label: No school facilities agenda stated
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources do not show a stated position on school buildings or facilities.

stanceCards.label: Mixed-use development to broaden tax base
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, Layton said mixed-use projects can expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools.

stanceCards.label: Supports more housing and affordable requirements
stanceCards.detail: She argued Winchester still needs hundreds of subsidized housing units, encouraged creative distribution of housing across town, and said developers should hear affordable housing requirements early.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes clearer expectations in development review
stanceCards.detail: She said the board should use pre-application meetings to set expectations on affordable housing, aesthetics, and height when inviting downtown development.

evidence.title: Candidate profile describes civic and board experience
evidence.summary: Layton said she served as a Precinct 2 Town Meeting member, on the Master Plan Steering Committee, on the Conservation Commission, and on the Planning Board, where she chaired MBTA Communities Act zoning work and Main Street Mixed Use District adoption.

evidence.title: Forum highlights housing and zoning positions
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Layton said Winchester remained hundreds of subsidized units short of its housing goal, supported creative housing distribution across town, and called for early pre-application meetings to define affordable housing, aesthetics, and height expectations.

evidence.title: Forum comments on fiscal impact of mixed-use projects
evidence.summary: Layton said mixed-use projects expand the commercial tax base without adding students to schools.

evidence.title: Election-night report says Layton led Planning Board field
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reported that Layton led the Planning Board race and won one of two seats.

evidence.title: Official results show Layton reelected to Planning Board
evidence.summary: Official results list Keri Layton with 2,331 votes in the two-seat Planning Board race, the highest total among the three candidates.

name: Michelle Prior
background: Incumbent Select Board member with 15 years on Town Meeting representing Precinct 4, six years on the Finance Committee including as FY24 chair, and three years on the Select Board including two years as chair. She has also served on the Audit Advisory Committee, Retiree Health Insurance Advisory Committee, and the Wright-Locke Farm Conservancy board.
summary: Prior ran for a second Select Board term emphasizing town finance experience, long-term financial planning, added non-property-tax revenue, housing options for residents of different ages and incomes, and coordination across boards and committees. She won reelection in March 2026.
locationNote: Winchester town government candidate profile and election results.
priorRecordLabel: Incumbent Select Board chair and former Finance Committee chair
priorRecordSummary: Prior had already served three years on the Select Board, including two years as chair, before the 2026 election. Reporting on the race also described her as the incumbent chair and noted her earlier six years on the Finance Committee.

stanceCards.label: Supports expanding non-property-tax revenue
stanceCards.detail: She called for added revenue through commercial growth, better monetization of town assets, and fee reviews, and cited her own work comparing town fees and expanding transfer station access for outside haulers.

stanceCards.label: Frames housing as an affordability and aging issue
stanceCards.detail: She said the town should support housing development that serves different family configurations and offers options for seniors who want to remain in Winchester.

stanceCards.label: Preferred a tighter override ask tied to identified needs
stanceCards.detail: During override negotiations, she argued the board should not ask voters for money beyond what modeling showed was necessary, rather than build in extra cushion 'just in case.'

stanceCards.label: Included school facilities within broader capital planning
stanceCards.detail: The override package she helped negotiate included capital funding partly intended for school facilities, but the sources do not show a detailed standalone school facilities platform from her.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes process, planning, and cross-board coordination
stanceCards.detail: She highlighted long-term financial planning, communication across boards and committees, support for the town manager, and consensus-oriented governance.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines experience and priorities
evidence.summary: Prior said she had 15 years on Town Meeting, six years on the Finance Committee, and three years on the Select Board including two as chair. She highlighted non-property-tax revenue, housing for varied incomes and ages, communication across boards, and a long-term financial plan.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on override response if voters reject it
evidence.summary: Asked how she would approach cuts after an override failure, Prior said the town manager, superintendent, and Town Meeting would likely be the final decision-makers, and she would advise review of unfilled positions, retirement incentives, and modest staffing or hours reductions where feasible.

evidence.title: State of the Town minutes confirm leadership role
evidence.summary: Official minutes from a joint State of the Town meeting list Michelle Prior as Select Board chair and note the committee's working-group process, corroborating her leadership role during the period before the election.

evidence.title: Override negotiations show her position on sizing the ask
evidence.summary: During the February 2026 override negotiations, Prior argued against asking voters for more than modeled needs and warned against tying funds too narrowly to the health care stabilization fund because that would reduce flexibility for schools and municipal departments.

evidence.title: After override defeat, she urged listening before another ballot question
evidence.summary: Following the override loss, Prior said the town should learn why the override failed through focus groups, listening sessions, and surveys, while adding that she expected a new ballot question within the year.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage describes campaign emphasis
evidence.summary: Election-night reporting described Prior as the incumbent chair, a public-sector consultant with 35 years of experience and six years on the Finance Committee, and said she emphasized deliberate process and consensus-building during the campaign.

name: Nick Rossettos
background: Incumbent Planning Board member and Winchester High School graduate who says he grew up in town. He works as a fractional or consulting CFO for life sciences companies and has highlighted a longtime interest in Winchester’s historic design and architecture.
summary: Rossettos ran for reelection to the Planning Board in 2026 emphasizing experience with mixed-use and affordable housing approvals, MBTA Communities zoning work, design standards, and preserving Winchester’s character while adding housing. He was one of three candidates competing for two seats and did not win.
locationNote: Grew up in Winchester and graduated from Winchester High School.
priorRecordSummary: Rossettos described four years of work on mixed-use development, the unanimous approval of the Waterfield affordable housing mixed-use project, zoning to comply with MBTA 3A, and zoning work for North Main Street. A forum report also identified him as an incumbent and noted his liaison roles to the Design Review Committee and Historical Commission.
keyThemes: Commercial tax base alongside residential development

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable and inclusionary housing
stanceCards.detail: He highlighted the Waterfield affordable housing mixed-use development, inclusionary housing in zoning work, and efforts to secure inclusionary units at 10 Converse Place.

stanceCards.label: Focuses on design and neighborhood fit
stanceCards.detail: He said new buildings should complement Winchester’s established character and stressed design standards and thoughtful design review.

stanceCards.label: Sees mixed-use as part of tax-base growth
stanceCards.detail: He said zoning should encourage mixed-use development and increase the town’s tax base, and argued the town needs a combination of residential and other development.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes financial discipline
stanceCards.detail: He cited his CFO background and framed development choices in fiscal terms, including the profitability of residential development and the need for balance.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines Planning Board experience
evidence.summary: Rossettos said he had spent four years working on mixed-use development, including the unanimously approved Waterfield affordable housing project, MBTA 3A compliance zoning, North Main Street zoning, and design standards.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on growth and preservation
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Rossettos said growth and preservation are not mutually exclusive and centered his campaign on preserving downtown character while adding housing.

evidence.title: Forum comments on housing production and zoning
evidence.summary: He cited Waterfield, Cambridge Street, Washington and Swanton, and 10 Converse Place as examples tied to subsidized or inclusionary housing, and said some zoning regulations are complicated and confusing.

evidence.title: Forum comments on fiscal impacts
evidence.summary: Drawing on his CFO work, Rossettos said developers favor residential projects because they are profitable and argued the town needs a combination of development types.

evidence.title: Incumbent status and board liaison roles
evidence.summary: The forum coverage identified Rossettos as an incumbent and as liaison to the Design Review Committee and the Historical Commission.

evidence.title: 2026 Planning Board election results
evidence.summary: In the March 21, 2026 town election for two Planning Board seats, Keri Layton received 2,331 votes, Amy S. Beliveau 2,042, and Nicholas J. Rossettos 1,864; Rossettos was not elected.

name: Shamus Brady
background: Public-school administrator and educator; former Winchester School Committee member (2021–2024) and former Finance Committee member during the early COVID-19 period. In 2026 he ran for Select Board.
summary: Brady presented himself as a progressive candidate focused on town finance, school-budget knowledge, an operating override, affordability relief, and more active business recruitment. He emphasized prior service on the School Committee and Finance Committee and argued for public input if cuts became necessary.
locationNote: Winchester resident and parent of two boys in public school, according to his candidate profile.
priorRecordLabel: School Committee and Finance Committee record
priorRecordSummary: Brady cited service on the Winchester School Committee from 2021 to 2024 and on the Finance Committee during the early months of the COVID-19 pandemic. Meeting coverage shows him supporting school budget additions tied to student services and asking implementation questions during a literacy review process.

stanceCards.label: Supports operating override
stanceCards.detail: He said the town needs to pass an operating override to maintain current service levels and that he fully supported passage.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes school-budget expertise
stanceCards.detail: He said his work in public schools and his School Committee service gave him detailed knowledge of the school budget, which he described as the largest part of the town budget.

stanceCards.label: Calls for affordability relief
stanceCards.detail: He said many town taxes and fees are regressive and proposed a committee on affordability, including consideration of a residential tax exemption.

stanceCards.label: Wants more business-generated revenue
stanceCards.detail: He said the town should more actively recruit businesses and identify which kinds of businesses would generate the most revenue.

stanceCards.label: Public input on cuts
stanceCards.detail: If an override failed, he said he would ask department heads to identify essential services and possible cuts, then bring proposals to the public through written feedback and forums.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines budget, override, and affordability positions
evidence.summary: Brady said Winchester needs an operating override, supported passing it, proposed an affordability committee, and called for stronger business recruitment and public input on any service cuts.

evidence.title: Candidate profile cites prior public-service background
evidence.summary: Brady said he worked on school and district budgets since 2008, served on the Winchester School Committee from 2021 to 2024, and served on the Finance Committee during the early COVID-19 period.

evidence.title: School Committee budget discussion shows support for direct student initiatives
evidence.summary: At a 2024 School Committee meeting, Brady said he liked proposals that would serve students directly and highlighted the need for funding flexibility related to literacy curriculum work.

evidence.title: Literacy review meeting shows implementation-focused questioning
evidence.summary: During a 2024 literacy discussion, Brady asked whether the UFLI literacy resource was available to all K–2 teachers across schools.

evidence.title: Election-night coverage summarizes campaign message and result
evidence.summary: Local election coverage said Brady pushed for urgency, visible dissent, and new revenue streams, and reported that Michelle Prior defeated him in the Select Board race.

evidence.title: Official 2026 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Michelle Prior received 2,849 votes to Brady’s 1,159 in the Select Board race.

name: Anthea Brady
background: Incumbent Select Board member seeking re-election in 2025. Brady said she had lived in Winchester for 11 years, holds a Master of Public Policy, previously worked in the education sector supporting federal, state and local agencies, especially in special education and K-12 data, and currently works with large organizations on culture and compliance. She said she had served three years on the Select Board, including two as vice chair, and had also served on the Personnel Board and as a Town Meeting member in Precinct 1.
summary: Brady ran as an incumbent emphasizing continuity in town government, support for schools and capital needs, and a willingness to consider free cash or an override to address the town’s structural deficit. She also pointed to affordable housing, economic development and pedestrian safety as priorities. She won re-election in the 2025 Select Board race.
locationNote: Lives in the Muraco neighborhood with her husband and two children, according to her candidate profile.
priorRecordSummary: Brady said she had served three years on the Select Board and two years as vice chair. In May 2025 meeting minutes, she is listed as Select Board vice-chair. News coverage also quotes her speaking in support of the amended school budget at Town Meeting.

stanceCards.label: Supports maintaining school investment
stanceCards.detail: Brady said the school system is a critical part of town infrastructure and should be supported with high-quality teachers, curriculum, professional development and classroom supports. At Town Meeting, she spoke in support of the amended school budget and said the schools obviously need help with math and schedule compliance.

stanceCards.label: Open to an override
stanceCards.detail: In her candidate profile and at the candidate forum, Brady said the town was considering free cash or an operating override to address the structural deficit, and that an override might be necessary.

stanceCards.label: Favors development-based revenue growth
stanceCards.detail: Brady pointed to reopening at the MBTA station, mixed-use opportunities from North Main Street zoning changes, and development potential in the Holton Street area as ways to expand commercial activity and the tax base.

stanceCards.label: Supports affordable housing growth
stanceCards.detail: Brady said Winchester should advance both large- and small-scale affordable housing, cited several housing sites, and said the Affordable Housing Trust should help shape 160 Forest Street with neighborhood engagement.

stanceCards.label: Backs capital and family-focused facilities work
stanceCards.detail: Brady cited support for safety assessment and funding for new playgrounds at Leonard and Macdonald fields, accessible playground equipment, and said the town needs continuity around a capital plan.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Brady described her education and work background, said she was seeking re-election after three years on the Select Board, and highlighted support for schools, housing, development, playgrounds and pedestrian improvements.

evidence.title: Select Board forum on budget shortfall and override
evidence.summary: At the March 2025 candidate debate, Brady said the budget was critical because costs had risen, that free cash could help in the short run, and that an override might be necessary. She also called for continuity around a capital plan.

evidence.title: 2025 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Brady received 1,649 votes in the Select Board race, finishing second behind Paras Bhayani and winning one of two seats.

evidence.title: Election-night summary confirms re-election
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that the winners in the four-candidate Select Board race were Paras Bhayani and incumbent Anthea Brady.

evidence.title: Town Meeting remarks supporting school budget
evidence.summary: Coverage of Town Meeting quotes Brady saying the schools need help with math, the McCall schedule needs to come into compliance, and the town should make the investment now.

evidence.title: Official minutes list Brady as vice-chair after election
evidence.summary: May 30, 2025 State of the Town minutes list Anthea Brady as Select Board vice-chair, confirming her ongoing board role after the 2025 election.

name: Christian Nixon
background: Architect with two degrees from Rice University and an education policy fellowship from Northeastern University’s Institute for Educational Leadership. He said he oversees budgeting, design, and construction of capital projects for a nearby community and school district and has more than 25 years of experience in K-12 education and healthcare project delivery.
summary: Incumbent School Committee member and longtime Town Meeting member who ran for re-election in 2025 emphasizing school funding, long-term town revenue, facilities planning, labor relations, and Lincoln Elementary improvements. He lost in a three-candidate race for two seats.
locationNote: Has lived in Winchester for 26 years and listed extensive service on Winchester boards and committees, including School Committee since 2010.
priorRecordSummary: Nixon said he has served on the School Committee since 2010 and on Town Meeting since 2008, with additional service on capital planning, audit, facilities, and expansion working groups.

stanceCards.label: Advocated for FY26 funding case
stanceCards.detail: He listed making the case for Winchester Public Schools’ true FY26 funding needs as a top priority and pointed to use of the Special Education Reserve Fund to protect the FY25 operating budget.

stanceCards.label: Backed sustainable revenue planning
stanceCards.detail: He said he supports the State of the Town’s goals toward sustainable revenue models to meet long-term town and district needs.

stanceCards.label: Focused on facilities projects
stanceCards.detail: He prioritized submitting the New Muraco Statement of Interest, completing Phase II of the 10-Year Facilities Master Plan, and securing funding for Lincoln Elementary HVAC and sitework improvements.

stanceCards.label: Emphasized reserve-fund governance
stanceCards.detail: He described the Special Education Reserve Fund as a budget-management tool with a Select Board concurrence requirement that provides a check-and-balance system.

evidence.title: Candidate profile on background and priorities
evidence.summary: Nixon described his architecture and education-policy background, long tenure in Winchester government, and campaign priorities including FY26 funding, sustainable revenue, facilities planning, WEA concerns, bargaining, and Lincoln improvements.

evidence.title: Response on budget management
evidence.summary: He said the School Committee used the Special Education Reserve Fund to cover unbudgeted FY25 special education costs, preserving the rest of the budget, and described the fund’s Select Board concurrence requirement as a check and balance.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results
evidence.summary: In the School Committee race, Nixon received 975 votes, behind Stefanie Mnayarji and Karen Bolognese, the two elected candidates.

evidence.title: Election-night report confirms outcome
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that School Committee winners were Stefanie Mnayarji and incumbent Karen Maruyama Bolognese, while incumbent Christian Nixon received 975 votes.

name: Dorothy R. Simboli
background: Lawyer with commercial and residential real estate experience; Massachusetts and Pennsylvania bar member. Winchester resident for 25 years and mother of three adult children.
summary: Simboli ran for Select Board in 2025 emphasizing housing growth with historic preservation, business-friendly commercial development, updated school curriculum and student supports, and more transparent long-term fiscal planning amid the town’s structural deficit. She previously served on the Zoning Board of Appeals and Town Meeting.
locationNote: Winchester resident; active in local government through the Zoning Board of Appeals and Town Meeting.
priorRecordLabel: Zoning Board of Appeals chair and Town Meeting member
priorRecordSummary: Served as Winchester Zoning Board of Appeals chair from March 2024, after prior service as associate full member and associate member; also a Town Meeting member since 2016. In 2025 Town Meeting debate on a literacy pilot, she asked how the schools would measure success.
keyThemes: Education quality including literacy, math, professional development and special education
Capital planning for school and town facilities

stanceCards.label: Wants closer review of school spending
stanceCards.detail: Said the school budget is more than half of the town budget and called for a deeper review of line items, including special education costs, while maintaining essential services.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy improvement with accountability
stanceCards.detail: Identified literacy as a critical school issue in her campaign profile and, at Town Meeting, asked how the district would determine success for the literacy pilot.

stanceCards.label: Highlights capital planning needs
stanceCards.detail: Pointed to deferred capital projects and current and future school projects including Lynch and Muraco as reasons for sound fiscal policy and long-term planning.

stanceCards.label: Supports commercial tax-base growth
stanceCards.detail: Advocated making Winchester more business-friendly, streamlining permitting, and exploring parking solutions to support business activity and broaden revenue sources.

stanceCards.label: Open to a right-sized override
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, said the town should consider an override but that it needs to be properly sized and part of strategic planning.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines background and priorities
evidence.summary: Simboli described her legal and real estate background, local service on the Zoning Board of Appeals and Town Meeting, and campaign priorities on housing, education, fiscal stability, and commercial development.

evidence.title: Calls for deeper budget review amid structural deficit
evidence.summary: She said the town’s structural deficit requires detailed budget evaluation, suggested rebidding health insurance, and said the school budget and special education costs warrant a deep dive into line items.

evidence.title: Affordable housing and business-friendly development proposals
evidence.summary: She supported more collaborative affordable housing projects, described 160 Forest Street as an opportunity for affordable housing, and proposed streamlining permitting and addressing parking to encourage business growth.

evidence.title: Forum remarks on override and long-term planning
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Simboli said the town should consider an override if right-sized and strategically planned, and said she wanted earlier involvement in the budget process.

evidence.title: Town Meeting question on literacy pilot accountability
evidence.summary: During Town Meeting discussion of the literacy pilot, Simboli asked how the School Department would measure success after the pilot program.

evidence.title: 2025 Select Board election result
evidence.summary: Simboli received 806 votes in the four-candidate race for two Select Board seats and did not win election.

name: Jack LeMenager
background: Winchester resident since 1988; member of the Historical Commission since 2009 and chair for the past eight years; Town Meeting member for 15 years; member of the executive board of the Town Meeting Members Association; served on committees including the train station study committee and Community Preservation Act Advisory Committee; recently retired after work in management consulting and employee communications.
summary: LeMenager ran a write-in campaign for Planning Board, arguing that Winchester should encourage more commercial and residential mixed-use development while improving coordination among town boards and preserving the town’s architectural character. He won the open seat in the 2025 election.
priorRecordLabel: Historical Commission and Town Meeting experience
priorRecordSummary: His cited public record is in related local governance roles rather than on the Planning Board itself, including long service on the Historical Commission, Town Meeting, and town committees.
keyThemes: Encouraging mixed-use commercial and residential development
Better coordination among boards and commissions
Preserving architectural integrity while supporting growth

stanceCards.label: No school-facilities position in provided sources
stanceCards.detail: The provided sources focus on planning, development, and town-board coordination, not school facilities.

stanceCards.label: Inter-board coordination and process improvement
stanceCards.detail: He said he would prioritize more communication among boards and closer work with the Select Board to better anticipate opportunities and support appropriate development.

stanceCards.label: Supports more housing and mixed-use development
stanceCards.detail: He said Winchester should encourage more commercial and residential development and cited mixed-use districts as part of that goal, but the sources do not give a detailed affordability platform.

evidence.title: LeMenager on mixed-use development and board coordination
evidence.summary: He said Winchester should encourage more commercial and residential development, simplify the approval track where possible, and improve communication among boards and commissions while protecting architectural integrity.

evidence.title: Background in local government and preservation
evidence.summary: The profile says he has served on the Historical Commission since 2009, chaired it for eight years, served 15 years in Town Meeting, and worked on committees including the train station study committee and CPA Advisory Committee.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results for Planning Board
evidence.summary: Official results show Jack LeMenager received 739 write-in votes for Planning Board, ahead of Matthew Zarracina with 146 and other write-ins with 129.

evidence.title: Election-night certification report
evidence.summary: Winchester News reported that certified final results showed LeMenager won the open Planning Board seat being vacated by Diab Jerius with 739 write-in votes.

name: Karen Maruyama Bolognese
background: Large animal veterinarian licensed in Massachusetts with prior finance, translation, and teaching experience; holds a DVM from Tufts, an MBA from the University of Michigan Ross School of Business, and a BA in English from Vanderbilt. Has lived in Winchester for 14 years.
summary: Incumbent School Committee chair and member since 2019, reelected in 2025. She emphasizes long-term planning, fiscal sustainability, and supporting opportunities for all students across academics, arts, sports, and special education.
locationNote: Lives in Winchester; reported 14 years in town.
priorRecordLabel: School Committee chair and incumbent member
priorRecordSummary: Served as School Committee chair in 2021-22 and 2024-25, and as a member since 2019. Also served as a Town Meeting member since 2012 and on several town working groups and committees.
keyThemes: family partnership on student technology use

stanceCards.label: Long-term budget planning
stanceCards.detail: Advocates a long-term, planful budgeting approach and said she wants the district and town collaborating on the five-year Winchester 2030 strategic plan and budget.

stanceCards.label: Tax burden awareness
stanceCards.detail: Said she takes fiscal responsibility seriously and recognizes the tax burden on residents while aiming to keep schools financially sustainable over the long term.

stanceCards.label: Supports superintendent's literacy process
stanceCards.detail: Backed the superintendent's literacy approach centered on elementary educators, leaders, and literacy specialists reviewing materials, assessments, and professional development before bringing recommendations to the committee.

stanceCards.label: Education and family partnership over blanket bans
stanceCards.detail: At the candidate forum, said phones are expected to stay in backpacks and smartwatches in school mode, but argued enforcement is difficult and emphasized education about wise usage and partnering with families.

stanceCards.label: Priority-setting through district process
stanceCards.detail: Said budget decisions should follow superintendent recommendations and School Committee review of prioritized district needs rather than promises about protecting or cutting specific programs.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: background and priorities
evidence.summary: Described her professional and educational background, School Committee service since 2019, Town Meeting service since 2012, and focus on meeting diverse student needs while balancing fiscal responsibility and long-term sustainability.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: budgeting approach
evidence.summary: Said she has advocated for a long-term, planful approach to budgeting and wants to help develop the five-year strategic plan and budget, Winchester 2030.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: literacy curriculum update
evidence.summary: Said she fully supports the superintendent's literacy process, with elementary educators and specialists reviewing and piloting options in 2025-26 before recommendations come to the School Committee.

evidence.title: Candidate forum: device policy comments
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, said district practice is that phones stay in backpacks and smartwatches be set to school mode; she said a total ban would be difficult and stressed education about wise usage and family partnership.

evidence.title: Official minutes list her as School Committee chair
evidence.summary: May 30, 2025 State of the Town meeting minutes list Karen Maruyama Bolognese as School Committee chair.

evidence.title: 2025 election result
evidence.summary: Official results show Stefanie Mnayarji and Karen Bolognese won the two School Committee seats; Bolognese received 1,512 votes.

name: Paras Bhayani
background: Corporate finance and business strategy professional; previously managed a $1.7 billion operating and capital budget for the Chicago Transit Authority. Holds degrees from Harvard College and Harvard Business School. Winchester resident for five years with two young daughters.
summary: Bhayani ran for Select Board emphasizing fiscal stewardship, long-term capital planning, housing growth on already developed land, economic development, and climate/sustainability. His profile and forum comments focused heavily on balancing the town and school budget through efficiencies plus likely new revenue, including possible override support.
priorRecordLabel: Education and policy involvement, but no prior Winchester elected office cited
priorRecordSummary: He said he had not held Winchester town office, but described himself as a founding member of the Winchester Literacy Initiative for Change and cited state-level board and policy work.
keyThemes: Fiscal stewardship and balanced town/school funding

stanceCards.label: Balanced funding approach for town and schools
stanceCards.detail: Said the town faces a challenging budget situation and would need efficiencies plus new revenue to protect services and fiscal health.

stanceCards.label: Likely support for additional override revenue
stanceCards.detail: Said the 2019 override had stretched beyond its intended duration and that additional revenue would likely be needed for operating and capital needs; at the candidate forum he said taxpayers would likely need to provide more money after efficiencies are found.

stanceCards.label: Use technology to improve town operations
stanceCards.detail: Proposed off-the-shelf technology to automate processes and digitize approvals, permits, and applications.

stanceCards.label: Supports infill and affordable housing on developed land
stanceCards.detail: Backed thoughtful infill development, completion of existing housing projects, and redevelopment of already developed sites while avoiding wooded land and open space.

stanceCards.label: Expand revenue through economic development
stanceCards.detail: Proposed mixed-use development, parking solutions, streamlined business processes, and maintaining the current real-estate classification system to support small businesses and broaden the tax base.

evidence.title: Candidate profile outlines professional background and priorities
evidence.summary: Bhayani described corporate and public-sector finance experience, literacy advocacy, and priorities including balanced funding, capital projects, housing, economic development, and sustainability.

evidence.title: Select Board candidate forum focused on budget shortfall
evidence.summary: At the March 5 forum, Bhayani said Winchester faced a challenging fiscal reality, called for a comprehensive long-term plan, said efficiencies should be identified first, and then taxpayers would likely need to provide additional money. He also referenced a capital-project override and a five-year capital plan.

evidence.title: Official 2025 election results
evidence.summary: Official results show Bhayani received 1,945 votes in the Select Board race, the highest total among the four candidates for two seats.

evidence.title: Election-night reporting confirms victory
evidence.summary: Local election coverage reported that Bhayani and incumbent Anthea Brady won the two Select Board seats.

evidence.title: State of the Town minutes show he served on the Select Board
evidence.summary: May 30, 2025 joint meeting minutes list Paras Bhayani as present as a Select Board member.

name: Rick Welch
background: Real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including permitting, developing, and building multiple 40B projects across Massachusetts. He said he has served in the past decade as president of Mesiti Development Corporation, developing senior living units.
summary: Welch presented himself as a development-focused Select Board candidate emphasizing affordable housing, senior housing, downtown mixed-use development, and protecting school and sports programs while the town addresses its budget deficit. In the 2025 Select Board race, he did not win one of the two seats.
locationNote: He said he has lived in Winchester for 35 years and that his three children graduated from Winchester Public Schools.
priorRecordSummary: Welch's profile cites extensive experience in real estate development, including affordable housing and senior living projects, and says he has worked closely with town officials and boards. The sources do not show prior elected service in Winchester.

stanceCards.label: Protect school and sports programs
stanceCards.detail: He said the town should find ways to address the deficit without touching school or sports programs and said he has observed excellence in the school system.

stanceCards.label: Grow tax base through development
stanceCards.detail: He proposed expanding the downtown center with lower-level commercial space and upper-floor retail/residential units, and supported incentives for 55-plus projects to bring in tax dollars.

stanceCards.label: Focus on affordable and senior housing
stanceCards.detail: He said his affordable housing background would help the town navigate housing needs and that he wants 160 Forest Street to offer opportunities for Winchester seniors struggling to remain in town.

stanceCards.label: Concern about structural deficit
stanceCards.detail: He described the increased budget deficit as concerning, but the provided sources do not show him endorsing a specific override or other named fiscal remedy.

stanceCards.label: Use development experience on town real estate deals
stanceCards.detail: He said he wants to use his development experience to help the town with multiple real estate deals while addressing affordable housing and senior housing needs.

evidence.title: Welch describes professional background
evidence.summary: He said he is a real estate developer and home builder with 35 years of experience, including 40B projects and senior living development.

evidence.title: Welch on budget deficit and schools
evidence.summary: He said the town needs to look closely at ways to make up for the deficit without touching school or sports programs.

evidence.title: Welch on affordable housing and 160 Forest Street
evidence.summary: He said his affordable housing experience could help Winchester's housing needs and that he would like 160 Forest Street to offer opportunity to local seniors.

evidence.title: Welch on downtown development and tax base
evidence.summary: He supported mixed-use downtown expansion, less parking in some projects, and incentives for 55-plus developments to grow the tax base.

evidence.title: Welch's local ties and motivation
evidence.summary: He said he has lived in Winchester for 35 years, his three children graduated from Winchester public schools, and he is running out of gratitude and a desire to contribute to the town's success.

evidence.title: 2025 Select Board election result
evidence.summary: Official results show Richard Welch, Jr. received 674 votes in the Select Board race, behind Paras Bhayani with 1,945 votes and Anthea Brady with 1,649 votes; two candidates were elected, so Welch lost.

name: Stefanie Mnayarji
background: Economist with degrees from the London School of Economics and Northeastern University; worked in FinTech consulting for large financial institutions and founded Luxxie Boston. Mnayarji said she has lived in Winchester for eight years.
summary: Mnayarji ran on school funding, curriculum, literacy reform, technology policy, and school culture. She emphasized protecting classroom investments, reviewing curriculum collaboratively, and tightening device use in schools. She won a School Committee seat in 2025.
locationNote: Mnayarji said she has lived in Winchester for eight years and was elected previously as a Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member.
priorRecordLabel: Town Meeting Member and parent/civic roles
priorRecordSummary: Before joining the School Committee, Mnayarji said she served as a Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member and held local parent and civic roles including co-chair of Parents' Inter-School Council, TMMA board, Ambrose School Council, and Ambrose PTO president.

stanceCards.label: Protect classroom investments
stanceCards.detail: She said budgeting should prioritize classroom size, student-teacher ratios, direct student services, literacy and math curriculum, teacher support, professional development, and special education, while seeking administrative efficiencies.

stanceCards.label: Supports literacy reform
stanceCards.detail: She identified literacy reform as a reason for running and called for curriculum review, pilot programs, classroom investments, and student-facing coaches in each elementary school.

stanceCards.label: Backs stricter device limits
stanceCards.detail: She called for intentional technology use and at the candidate forum said she supported a phone-free, more broadly device-free, school experience for students.

stanceCards.label: Links device limits to student well-being
stanceCards.detail: At the forum, she said phone-free districts report better focus and improved mental health.

stanceCards.label: Emphasizes transparent budget process
stanceCards.detail: She said budget decisions should be transparent and collaborative, with engagement from town boards, teachers, parents, and community members.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: professional and civic background
evidence.summary: Mnayarji described herself as an economist with FinTech consulting experience, founder of Luxxie Boston, eight-year Winchester resident, and Precinct 5 Town Meeting Member with several parent and civic roles.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: reasons for running
evidence.summary: She said she was motivated by funding, curriculum, and culture challenges, specifically literacy reform, broader curriculum review, intentional use of technology, device policy review, and fostering a culture where every voice is heard and every child thrives.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: budget approach
evidence.summary: She said the school budget should balance fiscal responsibility and educational quality through data-driven allocation, grant opportunities, partnerships, and transparent, collaborative decision-making.

evidence.title: Candidate profile: curriculum priorities
evidence.summary: She called for collaborative curriculum review, data analysis, pilot programs, feedback from students and parents, and investments including a student-facing coach in each elementary school and teacher professional development.

evidence.title: Candidate forum: device-free schools
evidence.summary: At the League of Women Voters forum, Mnayarji said she supported a phone-free experience and wanted to broaden it to device-free schools, arguing that communities must work together against addictive technology and that phone-free districts report better focus and improved mental health.

evidence.title: 2025 election result
evidence.summary: Official election results show Mnayarji received 1,964 votes in the School Committee race, the top total among the three candidates for two seats.
